code: @Manual{,   title = {ggpop: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)},   author = {Jorge A. Roa-Contreras and Ralitza Soultanova and Fernando Alarid-Escudero and Carlos Pineda-Antunez},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.6.0}, }

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: ggpop R package built top ggplot2 simplifies creation engaging, icon-based population charts. combining features ggplot2 ggimage, ggpop lets users easily visualize population data using proportional, customizable icons arranged intuitive, circular layouts. package also includes functionality adding clear, icon-enhanced captions, makes charts easier understand visually attractive. Designed primarily visual storytelling, ggpop helps users communicate complex population statistics straightforward appealing manner.

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: ggpop offers fresh alternative traditional data visualizations using icons proportional symbols population charts. approach improves aesthetics plots also helps audience better engage understand data. converting numerical values intuitive visual representations, ggpop makes complex population data clearer easier remember, allowing users tell compelling accessible stories.

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: Traditional charts can feel abstract. ggpop makes data human : Visual Impact: Icons create immediate connection Intuitive Understanding: Proportional representation simplifies complex data Flexible: Support 2,000+ Font Awesome icons + native optimized icons Fast: Optimized rendering handles 1,000 icons smoothly ggplot2 Native: Integrates seamlessly existing workflow

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: can install ggpop CRAN : Development version package can installed Github :

what: 1.- Create a Small Dataset or Use a Built-in Dataset
title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: dataset df_pop_mx minimal example illustrating population counts sex Mexico 2024. following structure: sex: categorical variable indicating sex, two entries: "male" "female" n: numeric variable representing population size sex category. country: constant value "Mexico", indicating country observations belong . continent: constant value "America", indicating continent observations belong .
code: library(dplyr) library(ggpop)  df_pop_mx <- data.frame(sex = c("male", "female"),                         n = c(63459580, 67401427),                         country = "Mexico",                         continent = "America")

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: apply process_data() function population data df_pop_mx following parameters: group_var = sex: groups data sex (male/female). grouping variable sum_var = n: uses column n (population counts) group totals. variable summed calculate proportions. sample_size = 1000: generates 1,000 sampled records, proportionally allocated group. package allows sample size 1000. function calculates group proportions, performs sampling create new data frame (df_pop_mx_prop). row represents one draw 1,000 samples. Notable columns: type: group (male female) sampled. n: total population count corresponding group. prop: proportion group overall dataset.
code: df_pop_mx_prop <- process_data(data = df_pop_mx, group_var = sex, sum_var = n, sample_size = 1000)  head(df_pop_mx_prop)

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: , create new column called icon df_pop_mx_prop dataset. case_when() function checks row’s type (either “male” “female”) assigns matching value (“ggmale” “ggfemale”) icon column.
code: df_pop_mx_prop <- df_pop_mx_prop %>%    mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "male" ~ "male",     type == "female" ~ "female"))

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: package supports Font Awesome icons. Font Awesome icons offer greater flexibility broader icon set (2,000+ free icons). However, rendering icons can slower sample size large (e.g., 1,000+ observations) plot faceted. icons stored fontawesome package. thing need specify icon’s name. example, just sample 2,000 free icons available fontawesome package: can check full list icons Font Awesome website.

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: Now can proceed plot population chart using assigned icons. geom_pop() function creates population chart using df_prop_mx_f dataset. object work geom_point() figure plotted determined x y coordinates. can also group color icons type variable varaible grouping .
code: library(ggplot2) ggplot() +   geom_pop(data = df_pop_mx_prop, aes(icon = icon, group=type, color=type),            size = 1, arrange=F, legend_icons=F) +   theme_void() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
text: Like ggplot object, can improve presentable plot. can arrange icons, add part legend, give color background, add title caption plot. can also include two icons plot. example, identify people disabled, change parameters.
code: ggplot() +     geom_pop(data = df_pop_mx_prop, aes(icon = icon, group=type, color=type),     size = 1, arrange=T) +     scale_legend_icon() + #add icon to the legend     theme_void(base_size = 40) +     theme(legend.position = "bottom") +  labs(title = "Population in Mexico by Sex",      subtitle = "2024",      caption = "Source: demogmx") +   theme(legend.title = element_blank(),         plot.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),         panel.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),         legend.background = element_rect(fill = "black"),         legend.text = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.title = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "white"),         plot.caption = element_text(color = "white")) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60"),                      labels = c("female" = "Females: 51%", "male" = "Males: 49%")) #1.- We load or create the data df_pop_dis_mx <- data.frame(sex = c("male", "female", "disabled males", "disabled females"),                         value = c(53726732, 54978806, 9731396, 11106712),                         country = "Mexico",                         continent = "America")  #2.- We process the data df_pop_dis_mx_prop <- process_data(data = df_pop_dis_mx, group_var = sex,                                 sum_var = value, sample_size = 500)  #3.- Assign icons to groups df_pop_dis_mx_prop <- df_pop_dis_mx_prop %>%    mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "male" ~ "male",     type == "female" ~ "female",     type == "disabled males" ~ "wheelchair",     type == "disabled females" ~ "wheelchair"))  #4.- Plot   ggplot() +   geom_pop(data = df_pop_dis_mx_prop, aes(icon = icon, group = type, color = type),            size = 1.3, arrange = T) +   scale_legend_icon(size = 10) +   theme_void(base_size = 36) +   labs(title = "Population in Mexico by Sex and disability status",        subtitle = "2022",        caption = "As of 2023, 16% of the population in Mexico has some form of disability.") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom",legend.title = element_blank()) +   scale_color_manual(values = c("male" = "#1E88E5", "female" = "#D81B60",                                 "disabled males" = "#90CAF9",                                  "disabled females" = "#F48FB1"),                      labels = c("male" = "Males", "female" = "Females",                                  "disabled females" = "Disabled Females",                                 "disabled males" = "Disabled Males"))

previous_headings: Quick Start > 6.- More examples employing facets and other packages
title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
code: # Example: Transportation Methods Across Cities with 7 Icon Groups  library(ggplot2) library(dplyr)  # 1. Create sample data for transportation methods across different cities df_transport <- data.frame(   method = rep(c("car", "bus", "train", "bicycle", "motorcycle", "walking", "taxi"), 5),   value = c(     # New York     45000, 32000, 28000, 15000, 8000, 25000, 18000,     # Los Angeles     62000, 28000, 12000, 10000, 12000, 15000, 22000,     # Chicago     38000, 35000, 30000, 12000, 6000, 22000, 15000,     # Houston     58000, 25000, 8000, 8000, 14000, 12000, 16000,     # San Francisco     35000, 30000, 25000, 22000, 10000, 20000, 20000   ),   city = rep(c("New York", "Los Angeles", "Chicago", "Houston", "San Francisco"), each = 7) )  # 2. Process the data for each city df_transport_prop <- process_data(   data = df_transport,    group_var = method,    sum_var = value,    sample_size = 400,   high_group_var = "city" )  # 3. Assign Font Awesome icons to transportation methods df_transport_prop <- df_transport_prop %>%    mutate(icon = case_when(     type == "car" ~ "car",     type == "bus" ~ "bus",     type == "train" ~ "train",     type == "bicycle" ~ "bicycle",     type == "motorcycle" ~ "motorcycle",     type == "walking" ~ "person-walking",     type == "taxi" ~ "taxi"   ))  # 4. Plot with facet_wrap ggplot() +   geom_pop(     data = df_transport_prop,      aes(icon = icon, group = type, color = type),     size = 1,      arrange = TRUE   ) +   facet_wrap(~ group, ncol = 2) +   scale_legend_icon(size = 6) +   theme_void(base_size = 26) +   labs(     title = "Primary Transportation Methods Across Major US Cities",     subtitle = "Distribution of daily commuters by transportation type",     caption = "Each icon represents approximately 400 commuters | Data for demonstration purposes"   ) +   theme(     legend.position = "bottom",     legend.title = element_blank(),     plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#1a1a1a"),     panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#1a1a1a"),     strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13, color = "white"),     legend.background = element_rect(fill = "#1a1a1a"),     legend.text = element_text(color = "white", size = 10),     plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", color = "white"),     plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "#cccccc"),     plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "#999999", size = 9)   ) +   scale_color_manual(     values = c(       "car" = "#E53935",       "bus" = "#FB8C00",       "train" = "#43A047",       "bicycle" = "#00ACC1",       "motorcycle" = "#8E24AA",       "walking" = "#FDD835",       "taxi" = "#FFB300"     ),     labels = c(       "car" = "Car",       "bus" = "Bus",       "train" = "Train/Subway",       "bicycle" = "Bicycle",       "motorcycle" = "Motorcycle",       "walking" = "Walking",       "taxi" = "Taxi/Ride-share"     )   )

previous_headings: Quick Start > 6.- More examples employing facets and other packages
title: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop)
code: # ---- Load hexgrid (for reference only) ---- my_sf <- read_sf("us_states_hexgrid.geojson") %>%   mutate(     google_name = gsub(" \\(United States\\)", "", google_name)   )  stopifnot("iso3166_2" %in% names(my_sf))  states_in_hex <- my_sf %>%   st_drop_geometry() %>%   transmute(state = iso3166_2) %>%   distinct() # ---- Real state-level infant mortality rates ---- # Data from CDC National Vital Statistics System, 2023 # Infant deaths per 1,000 live births  # ---- Real state-level gun death rates ---- # Data from CDC/Violence Policy Center, 2023 # Gun deaths per 100,000 population (all causes: homicide, suicide, unintentional)  df_rates <- states_in_hex %>%   mutate(gun_death_rate_per_100k = case_when(     state == "AL" ~ 25.6,     state == "AK" ~ 23.5,     state == "AZ" ~ 18.5,     state == "AR" ~ 21.9,     state == "CA" ~ 8.0,     state == "CO" ~ 16.6,     state == "CT" ~ 6.2,     state == "DE" ~ 12.0,     state == "FL" ~ 13.7,     state == "GA" ~ 18.6,     state == "HI" ~ 4.9,     state == "ID" ~ 17.9,     state == "IL" ~ 13.5,     state == "IN" ~ 18.3,     state == "IA" ~ 10.5,     state == "KS" ~ 16.3,     state == "KY" ~ 18.4,     state == "LA" ~ 28.3,     state == "ME" ~ 14.0,     state == "MD" ~ 12.3,     state == "MA" ~ 3.7,  # Lowest     state == "MI" ~ 13.9,     state == "MN" ~ 8.9,     state == "MS" ~ 29.4,  # Highest     state == "MO" ~ 21.4,     state == "MT" ~ 21.5,     state == "NE" ~ 10.6,     state == "NV" ~ 18.4,     state == "NH" ~ 9.6,     state == "NJ" ~ 4.6,     state == "NM" ~ 25.3,     state == "NY" ~ 4.7,     state == "NC" ~ 16.4,     state == "ND" ~ 12.8,     state == "OH" ~ 15.0,     state == "OK" ~ 19.9,     state == "OR" ~ 14.2,     state == "PA" ~ 13.6,     state == "RI" ~ 4.8,     state == "SC" ~ 19.1,     state == "SD" ~ 12.3,     state == "TN" ~ 22.0,     state == "TX" ~ 14.9,     state == "UT" ~ 14.8,     state == "VT" ~ 12.0,     state == "VA" ~ 13.8,     state == "WA" ~ 13.0,     state == "WV" ~ 16.8,     state == "WI" ~ 12.7,     state == "WY" ~ 21.5,     state == "DC" ~ 28.5,  # DC has very high rate     TRUE ~ 13.7  # US average as fallback   )) %>%   # Convert rate per 100,000 to expected cases per 100 people   # Rate is per 100,000, so divide by 1,000 to get per 100   mutate(     cases_per_100 = round(gun_death_rate_per_100k / 1000, 1),     # Convert to probability for binomial sampling     gun_death_rate = gun_death_rate_per_100k / 100000   ) # ---- Real state-level gun death rates ---- # Data from CDC/Violence Policy Center, 2023 # Gun deaths per 100,000 population (all causes: homicide, suicide, unintentional) # Each icon represents 1,000 people, so 100 icons = 100,000 people  df_rates <- states_in_hex %>%   mutate(gun_death_rate_per_100k = case_when(     state == "AL" ~ 25.6,     state == "AK" ~ 23.5,     state == "AZ" ~ 18.5,     state == "AR" ~ 21.9,     state == "CA" ~ 8.0,     state == "CO" ~ 16.6,     state == "CT" ~ 6.2,     state == "DE" ~ 12.0,     state == "FL" ~ 13.7,     state == "GA" ~ 18.6,     state == "HI" ~ 4.9,     state == "ID" ~ 17.9,     state == "IL" ~ 13.5,     state == "IN" ~ 18.3,     state == "IA" ~ 10.5,     state == "KS" ~ 16.3,     state == "KY" ~ 18.4,     state == "LA" ~ 28.3,     state == "ME" ~ 14.0,     state == "MD" ~ 12.3,     state == "MA" ~ 3.7,  # Lowest     state == "MI" ~ 13.9,     state == "MN" ~ 8.9,     state == "MS" ~ 29.4,  # Highest     state == "MO" ~ 21.4,     state == "MT" ~ 21.5,     state == "NE" ~ 10.6,     state == "NV" ~ 18.4,     state == "NH" ~ 9.6,     state == "NJ" ~ 4.6,     state == "NM" ~ 25.3,     state == "NY" ~ 4.7,     state == "NC" ~ 16.4,     state == "ND" ~ 12.8,     state == "OH" ~ 15.0,     state == "OK" ~ 19.9,     state == "OR" ~ 14.2,     state == "PA" ~ 13.6,     state == "RI" ~ 4.8,     state == "SC" ~ 19.1,     state == "SD" ~ 12.3,     state == "TN" ~ 22.0,     state == "TX" ~ 14.9,     state == "UT" ~ 14.8,     state == "VT" ~ 12.0,     state == "VA" ~ 13.8,     state == "WA" ~ 13.0,     state == "WV" ~ 16.8,     state == "WI" ~ 12.7,     state == "WY" ~ 21.5,     state == "DC" ~ 28.5,     TRUE ~ 13.7  # US average as fallback   )) %>%   # Since each icon = 1,000 people and we have 100 icons (100,000 people)   # The rate per 100,000 is exactly the number of affected icons   mutate(     expected_deaths_per_100_icons = gun_death_rate_per_100k,     # Round to get whole number of deaths     deaths_count = round(gun_death_rate_per_100k)   )  # ---- Create 100-icon samples per state ---- # Each icon represents 1,000 people df_people <- df_rates %>%   group_by(state, deaths_count, gun_death_rate_per_100k) %>%   summarise(     # Create 100 icons: deaths_count will be "gun death", rest will be "survived"     icon_id = 1:100,     status = if_else(icon_id <= unique(deaths_count), "gun death", "no gun death"),     .groups = "drop"   )  # ---- Convert to ggpop format ---- df_hex_prop <- process_data(   data = df_people,   group_var = status,   sum_var = NULL,   sample_size = 50,   high_group_var = "state" )  df_hex_prop <- df_hex_prop %>%   mutate(     icon = case_when(       type == "gun death" ~ "skull",       type == "no gun death" ~ "person",       TRUE ~ "person"     )   ) %>%   rename(code = group)  # ---- Plot using facet_geo ---- # ---- Plot using facet_geo with black background and enhanced design ---- # ---- Plot using facet_geo with black background and enhanced design ---- ggplot(df_hex_prop, aes(icon = icon, group = type, color = type)) +   geom_pop(size = 4, arrange = TRUE, facet = "code") +  # Increased size since fewer icons   facet_geo(~ code, grid = "us_state_grid3", label = "name") +   scale_color_manual(     values = c("gun death" = "#FF1744", "no gun death" = "#42A5F5"),     labels = c("Gun death (each skull = 2,000 people)", "No gun death")   ) +   theme_void(base_size = 14) +   labs(     title = "GUN VIOLENCE ACROSS AMERICA",     subtitle = "Each icon represents 2,000 people • Skulls show gun deaths per 100,000 population\nMississippi has nearly 8× the gun death rate of Massachusetts",     caption = "Data: CDC/Violence Policy Center, 2023 (age-adjusted rates: homicide, suicide, accidents)\nHighest: Mississippi (29.4 per 100k = ~15 skulls) • Lowest: Massachusetts (3.7 per 100k = ~2 skulls) • National Average: 13.7 per 100k"   ) +   theme(     # Black background     plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#000000", color = NA),     panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#000000", color = NA),          # Legend styling     legend.position = "bottom",     legend.title = element_blank(),     legend.text = element_text(color = "#FFFFFF", size = 16, face = "bold"),     legend.background = element_rect(fill = "#000000", color = NA),     legend.key = element_rect(fill = "#000000", color = NA),     legend.margin = margin(t = 15, b = 5),          # State labels     strip.text = element_text(       size = 12,        color = "#FFFFFF",       margin = margin(b = 4)     ),          # Title styling     plot.title = element_text(       hjust = 0.5,        face = "bold",        size = 24,        color = "#FF1744",       margin = margin(b = 10),       family = "sans"     ),          # Subtitle styling     plot.subtitle = element_text(       hjust = 0.5,        size = 13,        lineheight = 1.3,       color = "#E0E0E0",       margin = margin(b = 15)     ),          # Caption styling     plot.caption = element_text(       hjust = 0.5,        size = 9.5,        color = "#9E9E9E",        lineheight = 1.4,       margin = margin(t = 15)     ),     plot.margin = margin(t = 40, r = 40, b = 40, l = 40)          # Overall plot margins    )

what: Add Custom Captions with Icons to a geom_pop object — caption_pop
title: Add Custom Captions with Icons to a geom_pop object — caption_pop
text: caption_pop function allows add custom captions geom_pop object. function generates caption based images used plot. caption includes number individuals represented image. function also allows specify size caption text icons used caption.

title: Add Custom Captions with Icons to a geom_pop object — caption_pop
Add Custom Captions with Icons to a geom_pop object — caption_pop
text: caption_size Numeric. font size caption text. Default 3. icon_size Numeric. width images (icons) pixels within caption. Default 20. hjust Numeric. horizontal justification caption. Values range 0 (left) 1 (right). Default 0.5. text Named list named vector. Custom text descriptions icon. names correspond image identifiers plot data. NULL, defaults "persons".

what: Key drawing function for population-based image keys — draw_key_pop_image
title: Key drawing function for population-based image keys — draw_key_pop_image
text: Key drawing function population-based image keys

title: Key drawing function for population-based image keys — draw_key_pop_image
Key drawing function for population-based image keys — draw_key_pop_image
text: data data frame containing scaled aesthetics key. must include colour column color, alpha column transparency, icon column names icon files (without extension) used. params list additional parameters supplied geom. size width height key mm. value used directly function. stroke_width Numeric. Width black outline/border around icons pixels. NULL 0, outline drawn. Default NULL.

title: Key drawing function for population-based image keys — draw_key_pop_image
text: grid grob containing image icons specified colors transparency.

title: Key drawing function for population-based image keys — draw_key_pop_image
text: function creates custom key displaying population-based image icons legend ggplot2 plot. group can assigned different icon based information data$icon column, icons can colorized according specified colour alpha aesthetics. Optionally supports black outlines via stroke_width parameter. function relies ggimage:::color_image ggplot2:::ggname, internal functions. use necessary correct functionality, exported alternatives exist. acknowledge potential risks associated ::: usage, present, functions provide essential behavior rendering images within ggplot2. NOTE: Legend icons always rendered FIXED size, regardless size aesthetic mapped plot. ensures consistent legend appearance. stroke_width provided, icons rendered directly FontAwesome's stroke parameter consistent appearance plot legend.

title: Fetches the df_coordinates_final Dataset — fetch_df_coordinates
text: Downloads caches df_coordinates_final dataset already cached locally. function ensures dataset downloaded loaded memory without cluttering global environment. dataset stored package-specific cache directory retrieved efficiently subsequent uses.

title: Fetches the df_coordinates_final Dataset — fetch_df_coordinates
text: data frame containing df_coordinates_final dataset.

title: Fetches the df_coordinates_final Dataset — fetch_df_coordinates
text: dataset downloaded following URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jurjoroa/ggpopdata/main/data/df_coordinates_final.rda. file cached directory specific package, determined using R_user_dir. dataset already cached, loaded directly cache instead downloading .

what: Create a scatter plot with Font Awesome icons instead of points — geom_icon_point
title: Create a scatter plot with Font Awesome icons instead of points — geom_icon_point
text: Works exactly like geom_point(), renders Font Awesome icons instead dots. Pass data x y variables - special formatting required.

title: Create a scatter plot with Font Awesome icons instead of points — geom_icon_point
code: geom_icon_point(   mapping = NULL,   data = NULL,   stat = "identity",   position = "identity",   na.rm = FALSE,   inherit.aes = TRUE,   icon = NULL,   size = 3,   dpi = 50,   legend_icons = TRUE,   ... )

title: Create a scatter plot with Font Awesome icons instead of points — geom_icon_point
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). stat statistical transformation use data layer. using geom_*() function construct layer, stat argument can used override default coupling geoms stats. stat argument accepts following: Stat ggproto subclass, example StatCount. string naming stat. give stat string, strip function name stat_ prefix. example, use stat_count(), give stat "count". information ways specify stat, see layer stat documentation. position position adjustment use data layer. can used various ways, including prevent overplotting improving display. position argument accepts following: result calling position function, position_jitter(). method allows passing extra arguments position. string naming position adjustment. give position string, strip function name position_ prefix. example, use position_jitter(), give position "jitter". information ways specify position, see layer position documentation. na.rm logical, whether remove NA values inherit.aes FALSE, overrides default aesthetics, rather combining . useful helper functions define data aesthetics inherit behaviour default plot specification, e.g. annotation_borders(). icon Default Font Awesome icon (default: "circle"). size Default icon size (default: 3). dpi Icon resolution (default: 50). legend_icons Show icons legend (default: TRUE). ... additional parameters

title: Create a scatter plot with Font Awesome icons instead of points — geom_icon_point
text: ggplot layer.

title: Create a scatter plot with Font Awesome icons instead of points — geom_icon_point
text: geom_icon_point uses standard ggplot2 scatter plot aesthetics: x - Numeric variable x-axis y - Numeric variable y-axis icon - Font Awesome icon name (optional, column mapped) color/colour - Color grouping alpha - Transparency size - Icon size

what: Create a circular representative population chart — geom_pop
title: Create a circular representative population chart — geom_pop
text: Draws circular representative population chart based proportion groups, point (person) represents determined number individuals. Every person represented image given icon.

title: Create a circular representative population chart — geom_pop
code: geom_pop(   mapping = NULL,   data = NULL,   stat = "identity",   position = "identity",   na.rm = FALSE,   show.legend = NA,   inherit.aes = TRUE,   icon = "ggmale",   group_var = NULL,   sample_size = NULL,   arrange = FALSE,   seed = NULL,   sum_var = NULL,   facet = NULL,   size = 3,   dpi = 50,   legend_icons = TRUE,   stroke_width = NULL,   ... )

title: Create a circular representative population chart — geom_pop
text: mapping Set aesthetic mappings created aes(). specified inherit.aes = TRUE (default), combined default mapping top level plot. must supply mapping plot mapping. data data displayed layer. three options: NULL, default, data inherited plot data specified call ggplot(). data.frame, object, override plot data. objects fortified produce data frame. See fortify() variables created. function called single argument, plot data. return value must data.frame, used layer data. function can created formula (e.g. ~ head(.x, 10)). stat statistical transformation use data layer. using geom_*() function construct layer, stat argument can used override default coupling geoms stats. stat argument accepts following: Stat ggproto subclass, example StatCount. string naming stat. give stat string, strip function name stat_ prefix. example, use stat_count(), give stat "count". information ways specify stat, see layer stat documentation. position position adjustment use data layer. can used various ways, including prevent overplotting improving display. position argument accepts following: result calling position function, position_jitter(). method allows passing extra arguments position. string naming position adjustment. give position string, strip function name position_ prefix. example, use position_jitter(), give position "jitter". information ways specify position, see layer position documentation. na.rm logical, whether remove NA values show.legend logical. layer included legends? NA, default, includes aesthetics mapped. FALSE never includes, TRUE always includes. can also named logical vector finely select aesthetics display. include legend keys levels, even data exists, use TRUE.  NA, levels shown legend, unobserved levels omitted. inherit.aes FALSE, overrides default aesthetics, rather combining . useful helper functions define data aesthetics inherit behaviour default plot specification, e.g. annotation_borders(). icon icon used chart. group_var variable used group individuals. sample_size total number individuals (points) drawn. arrange Logical; TRUE, output data arranged group. seed Optional numeric seed used arrange = FALSE (randomized layouts). sum_var Optional variable sum instead counting. facet Optional facetting variable. NOTE: final plot must faceted; enforce validate_geom_pop_faceting(p) building ggplot object. size size points. dpi Height (pixels) PNG icon rendered fontawesome::fa_png(). Higher values produce sharper icons. Defaults 50. affects image dpi, icon size plot. legend_icons Logical; TRUE, legend display selected icons user. stroke_width Numeric. Width black outline/border around icons pixels. ... additional parameters

title: Create a circular representative population chart — geom_pop
text: geom_pop employs following aesthetics: sample_size - number individuals represented chart. alpha - transparency points. color - color points. size - size points.

title: ggpop: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop) — ggpop-package
text: ggpop R package creates visually engaging informative population charts. ggpop allows users represent population data proportionally using customizable icons, facilitating creation circular representative population charts.

title: ggpop: Visualizing Population Data (ggpop) — ggpop-package
text: Maintainer: Jorge . Roa-Contreras jorgeroa@stanford.edu (ORCID) Authors: Ralitza Soultanova Ralitza.soultanova@gmail.com (ORCID) Fernando Alarid-Escudero falarid@stanford.edu (ORCID) Carlos Pineda-Antunez cpinedaa@uw.edu (ORCID)

what: Process Population Data for Visualization — process_data
title: Process Population Data for Visualization — process_data
text: process_data function processes dataset calculate group proportions generates sampled dataset based specified parameters. processed data suitable creating visual representations, population charts, sample represents group associated counts proportions.

title: Process Population Data for Visualization — process_data
code: process_data(   data,   high_group_var = NULL,   group_var = NULL,   sum_var = NULL,   sample_size = 100 )

title: Process Population Data for Visualization — process_data
text: data data frame containing population data processed. high_group_var Character vector, optional. variables used group individuals hierarchically. categorical variable. provided, function samples individuals within group defined variables. group_var Quosure. variable used group individuals dataset. categorical variable. sum_var Quosure, optional. variable sum within group. NULL, function counts number individuals per group. sample_size Integer. total number individuals sample based group proportions. Must positive integer.

title: Process Population Data for Visualization — process_data
text: tibble (data frame) following columns: type sampled group type. group group identifier. n count individuals group. prop proportion group relative total population.

title: ggpop 1.6.0
text: release ggpop introduces significant enhancements including new geom function flexible data plotting, improved DPI handling, new customization options Font Awesome icons, multiple bug fixes enhance stability user experience.

title: ggpop 1.6.0
text: Fixed draw_key function prefer packaged PNG icons available cache icons temp directory, ensuring CI environment compatibility avoiding unnecessary regeneration (#189). Fixed arrange argument functionality geom_pop, ensuring proper icon arrangement behavior (#191). Fixed legend icon size rendering ensure consistent display across different scenarios (#199). Fixed facet_wrap bug ensure proper faceting behavior plots (#201). Fixed geom_pop work properly aesthetics specified main aes() call (#214). Fixed DPI parameter properly control icon rendering quality (#220). Fixed geom_icon_point DPI parameter wasn’t updating quality expected (#224). Fixed scale_legend_icon margin clipping issue prevent legend icons cut (#205). Fixed aspect ratio preservation icon rendering geom_pop draw_key_image functions (#230).

title: ggpop 1.6.0
text: Improved internal implementation removing reliance last_plot() pipeline geom_pop, enhancing code reliability avoiding potential side effects (#195). Enhanced unit tests improve code coverage reliability (#187). Improved README additional examples demonstrating package capabilities use cases, including faceting geofacet scenarios (#204). Updated unit tests robustness checks ensure stable behavior across different use cases (#216). Added comprehensive unit tests geom_icon_point ensure proper functionality (#223).

title: ggpop 1.6.0
text: Added new geom_icon_point function allow users plot data freely icons, providing greater flexibility beyond population-specific visualizations (#212). Added stroke_width parameter control thickness Font Awesome icons, enabling customization options (#217).

title: ggpop 1.5.1
text: release ggpop includes critical bug fixes improvements enhance stability, usability, documentation.

title: ggpop 1.5.1
text: Fixed draw_key function prefer packaged PNG icons available cache icons temp directory, ensuring CI environment compatibility avoiding unnecessary regeneration (#189). Fixed arrange argument functionality geom_pop, ensuring proper icon arrangement behavior (#191). Fixed legend icon size rendering ensure consistent display across different scenarios (#199). Fixed facet_wrap bug ensure proper faceting behavior plots (#201).

title: ggpop 1.5.1
text: Improved internal implementation removing reliance last_plot() pipeline geom_pop, enhancing code reliability avoiding potential side effects (#195). Enhanced scale_legend_icon documentation clarify serves wrapper guides, allowing users specify options directly (#115). Added comprehensive unit tests improve code coverage reliability (#187). Improved README additional examples demonstrating package capabilities use cases (#204).

title: ggpop 1.5.1
text: Issues listed chronological merge order. Issues related “Upload final set parameters” “Prerelease version vX.X.X” intentionally excluded. #189 #191 #195 #199 #201 #187 #115 #204
